title: Pain de campagne
subtitle: French country sourdough with a proportion of rye
excerpt: A French country loaf made with a blend of strong white flour and rye flour, leavened with a mature levain. The rye adds a mild earthiness and keeps the crumb moist; the long bulk fermentation develops flavour. Shaped as a boule and baked in a covered pot. Makes one large loaf.
sourceNotes: Pain de campagne is the traditional French country loaf, made with a blend of white and darker flour and naturally fermented. French breadmaking laws and guild records document naturally leavened country breads from the medieval period onward. The USDA public-domain publications on grain fermentation document wild-yeast leavening techniques consistent with this recipe. The specific formula — a levain of 20% flour weight, mixed white and rye flour, long bulk fermentation — is consistent with the published tradition of the French Compagnons Boulangers.

recipe.freezeNotes: Cool completely, slice, and freeze in a bag for up to 2 months. Toast from frozen.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: After shaping and placing in the banneton, cover and cold-prove in the fridge overnight (8–12 hours). Bake straight from the fridge, adding 5 minutes to the bake time.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven, lid on for first 20 minutes; remove lid and continue until the crust is a deep mahogany

glossaryTerms.definition: A portion of active sourdough starter refreshed with fresh flour and water and left to ferment for several hours before use. Building a fresh levain concentrates the wild yeast and bacteria activity and ensures a predictable, vigorous rise. A levain is ready when it has roughly doubled in volume, looks domed and bubbly, and a small spoonful dropped into water floats. The percentage given in a bread formula refers to the levain weight as a proportion of the total flour weight.

content.text: 4 hours before mixing: combine 50 g of your active {{sourdough-starter}} with 50 g {{strong-bread-flour}} and 50 g water. Cover and leave at room temperature. The levain is ready when doubled, domed, and a spoonful floats in water. The rye flour makes the dough slightly stickier than a pure white sourdough; resist adding more flour.

items.prepNote: room temperature; 340 ml for mixing, 25 ml for dissolving salt

content.text: Combine the {{strong-bread-flour}}, {{rye-flour}}, and 340 ml of the {{water}} in a large bowl. Mix until no dry flour remains. Cover and rest for 30 minutes.
Pour the ripe {{sourdough-starter}} over the autolysed dough. Dissolve the {{sea-salt-fine}} in the remaining 25 ml {{water}} and add that too. Incorporate with wet hands until no streaks remain.

Cover the bowl. During the first 2 hours, perform 4 sets of stretch-and-fold, one set every 30 minutes: reach under one side of the dough, stretch it up, and fold it over the top; turn the bowl 90 degrees and repeat four times. The dough will become smoother and more elastic with each set. Leave undisturbed for the remaining 2 hours until airy and increased by about 50%.

Flour the work surface lightly. Turn the dough out and pre-shape into a rough round; bench-rest for 20 minutes. Then shape into a tight boule: fold the edges into the centre, flip the dough over, and use cupped hands to drag it toward you to build surface tension. Place seam-side up in a well-floured banneton. Cover and prove at room temperature for 2 hours, or refrigerate overnight.

Preheat the oven to 240°C fan with a dutch oven inside for at least 45 minutes. Tip the loaf from the banneton onto a square of baking paper. Score the surface with a sharp knife or lame. Lift carefully into the hot pot, replace the lid, and bake for 20 minutes. Remove the lid and bake for a further 18 to 20 minutes until the crust is deeply coloured. Cool on a rack for at least 1 hour before cutting.
